item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: op i love this so much!! +1, very well written :D

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't like the letters at all, I don't like the writer of those letters either. He sounds like a needy man-baby that can't even brush his hair without his father's approval, that's pitiful. The hints are blatant but useless (One thinks they are there due to bad writing). The letters just don't work, they feel unnatural and childish, like if written by Pinnochio towards Gepetto. The item itself is interesting, but I feel like violins have been overdone (Maybe you could try with a magical digderidoo). To sum up, this scip should be rewritten. Downvoted

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: I just want to say "Oh hell yes."

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Timer Expired. Edits made as described below. Author may revert at anytime.

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team

+ Show Block
- Hide Block

It was mentioned up thread that there is the possibility that the piano mentioned in the most excellent tale Salut d'Amour may very well be the piano of "the master's son" from the notes included in this article. I would have to agree with that idea. As such, I am recommending the following edit:

Changing this:

The master's son and I have become great friends. He plays the piano better than anyone that I've ever seen. Up and down, his hands glide across the keys in such a beautiful way. He can play pieces with the order of a file of soldiers, and yet with the lightness of a butterfly.

To this:

The master's son and I have become great friends. He plays the piano better than anyone that I've ever seen. Up and down, his hands glide across the keys in such a beautiful way. He can play pieces with the order of a file of soldiers, and yet with the lightness of a butterfly.

If anyone has other/better examples that could be used here please leave a comment saying so!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
while a D-class who thinks he's the best will sound superior.

*wink.*

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yay!

Any particular reason that you didn't like it enough to upvote?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Seconding Dearheart.

Also the violin reminds me too much of La Corda d'Oro.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
…

…

…

Damnit. Right in the feels. This was awesome. But I already said that like fifteen times in chat.

So take all the +1's from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
On one hand, I kind of like the idea that the player gave the violin its power. It makes the addenda stronger emotionally, I think.

On the other hand, I went into the addenda assuming that the violin had its power in advance, and it definitely gave me a sense of foreboding over the course of the story that magnified its impact.

In conclusion: either way is good!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
^ And this is why I like it.

Well done, weizhong.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If you're confident, the violin will make you sound like a god. Otherwise, the less confident or the less happy you are, the less it improves your playing ability. So, Paganini could stroll in, and if he was extremely depressed, the violin would not improve his playing ability beyond his natural talent, while a D-class who thinks he's the best will sound superior.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The very blatant, nearly forceful attempt at emotional manipulation pulls the rug out from under it for me. When the intention is that obvious and direct throughout the whole piece, there's just no way I kin phil it.

The inclusion of the sheet music is cool, but the filefilefile thing was strange to read and I don't think it really gave the hint you intended.

The confidence/quality part seems barely anomalous. Is that not just…you know, pretty much how people work anyway?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It was simple and you described it well, flawless. +1

hope the file is not a virus… heh

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Is the piano mentioned here related to this at all? Even if it isn't, my headcanon says it is.. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I find that letting a story flow naturally makes for a more powerful narrative. Things happen because they make sense in the context of the story, making any sort of payoff or emotional investment feel earned and not manipulative.

As you've made extremely clear in the commentary, this was a mission to manipulate the feelings of the reader, and that addendum certainly felt every bit like someone grabbing my head with both hands and repeatedly going "see? you should feel SAD. SAAAAD." As a result, what's intended to be a moving experience feels to me to be a cloying, somewhat cynical exercise in playing on one of the more easily manipulated emotions.

I also feel like the equally open attempt to work easter eggs and whatnot into this detracted from the piece. I don't like stuff like that in general, because 99% of the time, the author isn't content to leave something subtle in there for a careful reader, for fear of it being not noticed. So when I read the word "file" a whole bunch of times in succession to the point that the writing feels stilted and unnatural, it doesn't do anything for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: No, that's the answer I personally prefer :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The former. I'm aware that a few people got confused, which was partially why I threw in that last note, both as the cherry on top and the clarification to help make it clear.

Does that change your opinion on it?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
well this was cute as shit

Question: The document can be interpreted either way (AFAIK), but I want the author's opinion — did the situation described in the letters give 1758 its anomalous properties, or did it have the properties before the narrator got it? I personally like to think it's the former.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The description had me at the edge of downvoting, but then those addenda. Oh god, the feels.

+1, you deserve it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You really pulled this together, weizhong. Those last two notes got my upvote. +1'd for making me feels.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Was thinking this to be rather dull, but with the recovered documents, it drives home how this thing could build a person up only to tear them down. Nicely played.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is utterly fantastic. A great treatment of a very simple idea.
+1

item.title: Author Overview
item.content:encoded:
Time for a feels-y skip.

The inspiration for this came from my desire to write a sadder, more emotional story, like the original tale that I wrote for this site. Lots of thanks to Bryx, Immersturm, ghostchibi, Kraito, Devereaux, and especially CryogenChaos for their help in looking over this. Special thanks goes to CryogenChaos for helping to inspire the note at the bottom, and his excellent critique that helped tie the thing together.

Now, did you catch the hidden reference?

Spoiler 1 for those who want a challenge
SPOILER ALERT

Did you notice a certain misplaced word that appeared in the letters very frequently?

Spoiler 2 for those who didn't get the first
SPOILER ALERT

Notice the use of the word "File." Now, where else can you find "File" on a wikidot page? *Wink wink*

Spoiler 3 for the lazy
SPOILER ALERT

Click the "Files" button at the bottom of the page.

General Spoiler: Check this one out when you're done with the ones above.
SPOILER ALERT

Read this link about the significance of this piece.
